The Municipal Mayor require periodic physical inventory of supplies and materials. The physical count of unissued inventory items by type shall be conducted at least once a year and reported in the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI).

Initiate the preparation of necessary documents to support cost estimate of projects funded under the 20% Development Plan before the budget preparation.
Develop and recommend other procedures to enhance the current practice regarding the planning and budgeting of the 20% Development Plan to avoid generic and lump sum projects.

We recommended that the Accounting Office submit immediately the disbursement vouchers together with the supporting documents for the months of October to December 2020 and payroll for the month of December 2020, henceforth strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009 on the submission of accounts and other reports of the municipality on or before the set deadline, to avoid administrative sanctions and/or disallowance of said accounts, as provided for in Section 39 (1) & (3) of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445.

We recommended that management strictly comply with the bonding requirement pursuant to Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 and pertinent provisions of RA No. 7160 to protect government funds against losses through force majeure.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. reconcile the balances of Cash in Bank-LCCA account per books and per
bank and effect the necessary adjustments; and
b. prepare adjusting entries for all identified reconciling items in the books
and for long outstanding and/or stale checks; Henceforth, record
immediately the reconciling items after preparation of the Bank
Reconciliation Statement/s.

We recommended that Management:
a) demand the immediate liquidation of overdue cash advances and cause the
withholding of the payment of salary of the AOs if after 30 days due
demand was made, no liquidation is received from the Accountable
Officer;
b) send demand letters to persons previously connected with the LGU who
still have unliquidated cash advances, otherwise officials responsible for
clearing said individuals from money accountability be held liable for the
unsettled cash advances;
c) instruct the Municipal Accountant to monitor the grant of cash advances
and enforce strictly the immediate liquidation thereof; and
d) stop the granting of additional cash advances unless previous cash
advances had been settled/liquidated.

We recommended that Management direct the:
a. Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the Municipal Assessor to make
the necessary update on the RPTAR to reflect the true and correct
amount of RPT and SET Receivables of the LGU;
b. Municipal Treasurer to monitor regularly the RPTAR and effect the
collections and advance payments collected to determine the outstanding
receivables as at year-end;
c. Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Accounting Office at the beginning
of the calendar year a list showing the name of the taxpayers and the
amount due and collectible for the year which will be the basis in setting
up the RPT and SET Receivables; and
d. Municipal Accountant to effect adequate recording under Real Property
Tax Receivable/Special Education Tax Receivable and Deferred Real
Property Tax Income/Deferred Special Education Tax Income accounts
and provide and maintain and updated subsidiary ledgers of the
accounts.

We recommended that Management: a) create a committee composed of one
personnel each from General Services and Accounting Office who shall reconcile
their respective records of PPE on a full time basis and at the same time maintain
updated and complete PPE Ledger Cards and Property Cards; and b) in ensuing
years, ensure that the conduct of physical count of PPE was in accordance with
COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, prescribing the guidelines and
procedures in the conduct of physical count of PPE, recognition of PPE items found
at station, and disposition for non-existing/missing PPE items, for the one-time
cleansing of PPE account balances of government agencies.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to
reconcile the reciprocal accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds
and draw the necessary journal entry to correct the balances.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to:
a) send confirmation letters to creditors to prove the existence of these
liability accounts; and
b) ensure that claims are substantiated with complete documentation,
otherwise revert said balances to Unappropriated Surplus account.

We recommended that Management:
a) allocate budget for the ₱14.89 million accrued payable for the terminal
leave benefits earned by its employees;
b) include in the Annual Budget every year the appropriation for the
accrued payable on vacation and sick leave benefits earned by each
employee per year to ensure that employee benefits are properly
accounted for;
c) set-up a special fund for the accrued earned leave of employees to
defray expenses of employees’ benefits in the future; and
d) direct the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting
entries to record the accrued liability pertaining to the earned leave
benefits of the employees after ensuring the correctness of the
51
accumulated leave credit balances of employees as of December 31,
2020.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to: a)
prepare and submit the fund liquidation reports pertaining to the completed
programs/projects and return the unexpended balances amounting to ₱0.59 million
to the source agencies in order that the account of the LGU in the books of the
source agencies are settled; and b) remit to the Comelec and Napolcom the fees
collected amounting to ₱93,535.50.

We further recommended that Management agreed to request and seek the
approval of the DBM on the realignment of the program/project under the BUB-
2016 stipulated in the MOA before starting the realigned program/project.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to:
a) prepare and submit the fund liquidation reports pertaining to the completed
programs/projects and return the unexpended balances amounting to ₱1.90 million
to the source agencies in order that the account of the LGU in the books of the
source agencies are settled; and b) compute the shares of other LGUs on monies
collected by the Municipality and remit the same to the concerned other LGU/s.

We recommended that Management through the Municipal Social Welfare
and Development Office: a) submit the lacking documents; and b) ensure that all
documentary requirements are complied with before release/distribution of SAP to
avoid audit sanctions.

We recommended that Management enter into a MOA with the DEPEd to
ensure that the donated equipment are recorded in the books of account and
accountability to the asset is established.

We recommended that Management require the BAC to judiciously plan the
procurement process and stop the practice of resorting to splitting of
requisitions/contracts to circumvent existing laws or regulations on procurement.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to
reconstruct the maintenance/repair record of individual motor vehicle owned by the
LGU so as to guide Management on arriving at a decision whether it’s still
economical to maintain subject vehicle/s continuously repaired.

We recommended and Management agreed to ensure that withholding taxes
are deducted from the monthly honoraria paid to its national government agency
officials/employees assigned in the LGU and remit the same to the Bureau of
Internal Revenue (BIR).

We recommended that management, thru the Accounting Office, reclassify the advance payment for mobilization fees recorded in Other Receivables account to Advances to Contractors account, in their respective fund, to conform with the Revised Local Government Chart of Accounts prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-09 dated December 1, 2015, for fair presentation of financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Reclassified/ recorded in the books.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management return immediately to the source NGAs the idle funds aggregating P 5,159,110.70 pursuant to Section 4 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994 and Section 3.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-01 dated June 14, 2012 on the submission, and liquidation of fund transfer received from National Government Agencies (NGAs) to make these funds available for appropriation to other priority programs, projects and activities of the government.
observations.managementAction: The DOH Salintubig Fund amounting to P5,039,061.60 was utilized in the construction of water system.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management fast track the completion of all on-going and unimplemented projects funded under the 20% Development Fund to optimize utilization thereof and fully deliver on time these benefits to the community. 

4.6.	 Likewise, the concerned department was advised to monitor regularly the implementation of the 20% Development Plan and closely coordinate with other departments to fully achieve the desired goals and benefits as planned.
observations.managementAction: Continuing appropriations were implemented/ realigned for priority programs

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the contractor to refund the amount of the deficiency or to deduct the amount from any claim of the contractor.
observations.managementAction: Notice of Disallowance representing liquidated damages and all deficiencies were deducted and settled by the contractor.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management:

Require the contractor to complete the construction of the NCDC project, otherwise, the municipality shall rescind the contract, forfeit the contractor's performance security and takeover the prosecution of the project or award the same to a qualified contractor through negotiated contract. Liquidated damages incurred shall be deducted from the claim of the contractor.

Require the contractor to complete the project of the Concreting of Tabba-Tupanna Road, otherwise, the municipality shall rescind the contract, forfeit the contractor's performance security and takeover the prosecution of the project or award the same to a qualified contractor through negotiated contract.  Liquidated damages incurred including the excess amount of the contract over ABC shall be deducted from the claim of the contractor.
observations.managementAction: Deficiencies were deducted from the claim of the contractor

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management formulate Policy Guidelines in hiring contract of service employees, taking into consideration the necessity as to the reasonable number of contract of service employees to be assigned in different offices in order to avoid excessive manpower, work duplication and unnecessary expenditures for wages.

 Ensure that the policy guidelines formulated would not, in any way, restrict the manpower requirements needed in the delivery of vital services for the implementation of the various programs and activities of the municipality but promote optimum utilization of financial resources.
observations.managementAction: The policy is being enhanced to incorporate  high need of job order employees to address disaster activities.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management stop assigning JO personnel as collecting/disbursing officers since no employer-employee relationship exists between the LGU and the JOs, and ensure strict adherence to existing laws and regulations that all accountable officers/employees shall be properly bonded to safeguard government funds.
observations.managementAction: JOs were still allowed to collect and disburse funds as collecting/disbursing officers because of limited number of regular and permanent personnel in the Office of the Municipal Treasurer.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer (a) review the RCDs and see to it that these are completely and properly filled up; (b) check the accuracy of the accountability for accountable forms by doing physical count of all accountable forms; and (c) carry forward the correct beginning balance to the succeeding RCDs.
observations.managementAction: None
observations.reason: The RCD was still properly filled up by the collectors.
Permanent logbook for cash tickets was still not properly maintained.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality appropriate and utilize the 20% Development Fund only for viable and sustainable development programs and projects enumerated under DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.
observations.reason: Based on the Annual Investment Program of the Municipality for CY 2019, there were still PPAs which are not among the priority projects enumerated under DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 which were still included therein.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MSWDO to carefully plan the procurement of food items for the Supplemental Feeding Program and for the BAC to undertake the procurement thru competitive bidding in accordance with Section 10, Rule IV of the IRR of RA No. 9184, except as provided in Rule XVI of the IRR.
observations.reason: The liquidation reports in CY 2019 relative to SFP were still not submitted to the Audit Team, hence, we could not validate whether or not procurement of food items were made through public bidding.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the MSWDO prepare the PRs for the procurement of food items based on the approved cycle menu (Annex A of AO No. 04, series of 2016). If there be any modification thereof, the procurement of the MSWDO is likewise required to attach in the DV the duly reviewed and approved menu by the MNAO/Nutritionists of the Field Office so that purchases of ingredients could be properly evaluated. We further recommended that the Accounting Office see to it that disbursement vouchers covering payments for the procured items are supported with complete documentation, including the daily attendance.
observations.managementAction: The liquidation reports in CY 2019 relative to SFP were still not submitted to the Audit Team, hence, we could not validate whether or not PRs for the procurement of food items were based on the approved cycle menu. However, the Municipal Accountant informed that the procurement of food items for the last cycle of the SFP were already undertaken by DSWD Field Office No. VII.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor prepare the special project plan showing the outputs or deliverables per project component of each members of the MCT to determine the estimated man-hours spent by the latter which shall serve as basis in the computation of their honorarium and the other aforementioned lacking documentary requirements required under COA Circular No. 2012-1. Henceforth, ensure that the disbursement vouchers and payrolls are supported with complete documentary requirements to establish the validity of the transactions made.
observations.reason: Management still did not submit the special project plan showing the outputs or deliverables per project component of each members of the MCT.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality optimally utilize its LDRRMF for properly chargeable expenditures that would strengthen its capacity to or mitigate the adverse effects of disasters or calamity as provided under RA No. 10121 and NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 2013-1.
observations.reason: There were still various expenditures which were not chargeable to LDRRMF which were charged thereto such as traveling expenses of personnel to Zambales to pick up ambulance, spare parts for the ambulance/rescue vehicles and wages of job order personnel assigned in the LDRRMO.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the SWMB review and update the MSWMP as required under Section 16 of RA No. 9003 for purposes of ensuring its sustainability, viability, effectiveness and relevance, and to submit the same to the NSWMC for review and approval.
observations.reason: The updating of the SWMP was still on-going.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Inventory Committee prioritize its conduct of physical inventory of properties and accomplish the necessary reports annually in accordance with Section 124 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer’s Office update the AREs and maintain property cards. Likewise, the Accounting Office should update the PPE ledger cards and the Schedule of PPE. Accordingly, the two offices should make a periodic reconciliation of these records.
observations.managementAction: Inventory count on PPEs of the Municipality was not completed, hence, Report on Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment was not prepared and submitted to the Auditor.
observations.reason: Moreover, in the absence of RCPPE, reconciliation between the account records and results of the actual count could not be made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management strictly observe and implement the regulations on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997. We further recommended that management demand from concerned employees for the immediate settlement of their cash advances.
observations.reason: Advances for Operating Expenses, Payroll and Officers and Employees still had an outstanding balance of ₱456,375.12 as of December 31, 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Chairman of the LCPC see to it that the Council perform its functions in accordance with the DILG MC No. 2005-07 dated February 1, 2005 to make the Municipality child-friendly.
observations.reason: The LCPC in the Municipality had not submitted the Records or Minutes of Meetings and Accomplishment Report, which are indicators that the LCPC was operational or functional, hence, we could not review and validate whether it was fully operational.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality enter into a MOA with the selected drugstores/pharmacies, providing therein the maximum allowable credit, the billing period and other conditions necessary, in accordance with Sections 6.1.8 and 8.1.2 of DSWD MC No. 04, S. 2015 dated March 6, 2015.
We recommended that management complies with the documentary requirements under Section 5.7.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-01 dated June 14, 2012 before granting honoraria to its officials and employees.
observations.reason: Personnel concerned were no longer in the government service.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Inventory Committee include supplies and materials when it conducts its physical count and for the offices of the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to maintain the required supplies ledger cards and stock cards, respectively. Furthermore, the Municipal Accountant should strictly follow the Perpetual Inventory Method of recording purchases of supplies and materials as provided in the NGAS Manual.

We recommended that: (a) Stop the practice of charging prior years’ electricity expenses against the appropriation of the year it was paid. (b) If funds are available, require the Sangguniang Bayan to make a supplemental budget so that unpaid obligations can be settled properly. Exercise prudence in spending the municipal funds. Exert effort to control the use of electricity so that the municipality can avoid problems in paying the obligation.
observations.reason: The Municipality still had prior years’ electricity expenses which were still charged against the current year appropriations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a. The Accounting Office and GSO reconcile their records of unserviceable properties. b. The Municipal Treasurer prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) with the appraisal of each item conducted by an appraisal committee created for the purpose. c. Management undertakes the immediate disposal of the unserviceable properties following the procedures and guidelines provided under Section 79 of Presidential Decree 1445 and Sections 501-510 of the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual, Volume I. The Municipal Accountant drop the amount of the unserviceable properties from their books of accounts after disposal, and records the proceeds of the auction sale, if any.
observations.reason: The inventory of movable properties was still on-going. The IRRUP was not prepared, hence, unserviceable properties were not disposed.

observations.recommendation: Required the officials and other personnel concerned to refund in full the amount received by them as they are not allowed in audit for lack of legal basis.
observations.reason: Personnel concerned were no longer in the government service.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management: a. Direct the Treasury, Accounting and Legal Offices to secure the Land Titles to all real properties as an indispensable requirement. Lands owned or acquired by LGUs without Torrens Title shall within the next fiscal year from their acquisition be asked to be surveyed with a view to securing title thereto, and the appropriation for such survey and other requirements for securing title shall be deemed a preferred charge against the unappropriated funds during the said ensuing fiscal year (Sec. 955 of COA Circular No. 92-386). b. Instruct the Legal Office to facilitate the application for the issuance of the TCT in favor of the Municipality of Pamplona so that government may be shielded from lawsuits or unlawful claims over such property. Subsequently, all purchases of real property should be done in accordance with the pertinent laws governing such transactions to prevent disallowances in the audit. Direct the Accountant and the Assessor to reconcile both their records so that these should come into agreement for the Land account to be fairly presented in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The documents required for securing land titles were not available. The incumbent Municipal Assessor found it difficult to locate the documents because there was no proper turnover from his predecessor. Further, the title number of these Lands properties are not reflected on the tax declarations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal officials concerned ensure that all real properties owned by the Municipal government are properly recorded and covered by valid documents showing legal ownership.
observations.reason: (Not Implemented in 2008 AAR)

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to strategically implement its developmental projects within the target period of accomplishment to ensure that the 20% of the IRA is optimally utilized in the prompt implementation of programs and projects to achieve the desirable socio-economic development as prescribed under DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular 2011-1.
observations.managementAction: A query was sent to  DBM for the measures that may be taken to implement the pending projects based on the grounds used by the SP in declaring the budget inoperative. Realigned non-feasible projects due to budget and time constraint.
observations.reason: Delay was due to the declaration by the Sangguniang Panlalawigan of the Annual Budget as inoperative.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer in coordination with the Municipal Assessor to make necessary update on the RPT Account Register of Individual Taxpayers to reflect the true and correct amount of RPT and SET Receivables of the LGU as of the period and monitor regularly to effect the collections and advance payments, if any, in order to determine the outstanding receivables as at the end of the period.

We likewise recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to record the corresponding RPT and SET Receivables based from the report or list furnished by the Municipal Treasurer as well as provide and maintain subsidiary ledgers of the accounts.
observations.managementAction: Designate personnel to handle said task
observations.reason: Delay was due to lack of manpower to handle the recording and updating of Individual Taxpayers’ Card; On –going revision of list, hence no summary list of RPT and SET Receivables were submitted for recording by the Accounting Office

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to ensure that the programs and projects covered by LDRRMF annual plan are implemented during the budget year to achieve its objectives.
observations.managementAction: Realigned non-feasible projects due to budget and time constraint.
observations.reason: Delay was due to the declaration by the Sangguniang Panlalawigan of the Annual Budget as inoperative.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to:

direct the Local School Board to revisit and consider any possible revision on the project proposal in order to timely address the concerns of the school-beneficiaries; and 

ensure the prompt and proper implementation of the project.
observations.managementAction: Include in the prioritization of programs by the DEPED during their School Board Meeting; Revision of Program
observations.reason: Insufficiency of Funds to implement the original proposal due to inflation in market prices; Non-inclusion in the prioritization of the Local School Board

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to require:

the Municipal Agriculturist to facilitate the completion and collation of  the necessary documents to formalize the transfer of the biological assets and other structures to the LGU and;  prepare and submit Quarterly Report of Biological Assets to the Accounting Office to facilitate proper valuation of the account; and 

the Municipal Accountant to effect the necessary adjustments to reflect the Biological Assets, Other Structures and income accounts and thenceforth strictly observe the provisions of Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standard concerning proper recognition, measurement and presentation of Biological Assets account.
observations.managementAction: Continuously requesting for MOA from DA together with the other supporting documents;
observations.reason: Lack supporting documents as basis for recording.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to: 

require the Disbursing Officer to immediately settle the unliquidated cash advances as of CY 2018 and strictly comply with the provisions of the aforecited circular;

undertake administrative measures for the settlement of unliquidated cash advances; and 

formulate measures and/or set-up controls to ensure the strict compliance with the rules prescribed on liquidation of cash advances.
observations.managementAction: Demand the Disbursing Officer to fully Liquidate the cash advances
observations.reason: The Disbursing Officer alleged that claims were already paid but payrolls were misplaced and cannot be retrieved to be forwarded to Accounting Office

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management appropriate fund that will finance the titling of the 37 parcels of land in the name of the Municipality to confirm its ownership.  

We also recommended that the Municipality consider the hiring/procurement of the services of reputable company that will conduct the survey and facilitate the fast processing of the Transfer Certificate Title of the 37 parcels of land in the name of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the former Acting Municipal Treasurer deposit
collections in CY 2019 amounting to ₱410,429.43 immediately. Henceforth, the
incumbent Acting Municipal Treasurer is required to deposit his collections
within the prescribed period in accordance with Section 69(1) of P.D. No. 1445
and Section 32 of the NGAS Manual, Volume I.

We recommended and the Acting Municipal Treasurer agreed to require
the collectors to remit immediately their collections totaling ₱273,634.92 and to,
henceforth, prepare the RCD and remit all collections daily to the designated LO.
We also recommended that she require the LO to verify the RCD by checking
the issued ORs as to serial numbers, dates, and total collections remitted as well
as count and check the AFs in the custody of the collectors against the balances
on hand in the report.

We further recommended that the collectors prepare and submit the
RAAFs monthly to enable the MT to monitor and account for the ORs issued
and the balances to serve as bases in the reconciliation and issuance of the next
series.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to require each
collector to prepare the RAAF monthly as basis for the preparation of the
CRAAF and submit a copy to the Auditor in accordance with Section 51 of the
MNGAS, Volume II. We further recommended that the Municipal Treasurer
make a physical count of the accountable forms remaining in their custody and
check the same against the balances on hand column of the RAAF.

We recommended and the incumbent Liquidating Officer/Acting
Municipal Treasurer agreed to (a) prepare the RCD at the close of each business
day; (b) update his cashbook daily based on the RCD; (c) reconcile cashbook
balances with the subsidiary records and cash on hand daily in accordance with
existing regulations; and (d) effect appropriate adjustments.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive or the Municipal
Treasurer properly designate a liquidating officer in accordance with CSC
Memorandum No. 06 s. 2005 dated February 15, 2005.

We recommended and Management agreed to strictly comply with
Sections 77 and 80 of P.D. No. 1445 to ensure proper turnover of government
funds and property from the outgoing to the incoming accountable officer.

We recommended and the incumbent Acting Municipal Treasurer agreed
to purchase cashbooks and require the collecting and liquidating officers to
maintain an official cashbook for all their collections and remittances in
accordance with Section 63 (B-C) of the Local Treasury Manual. Further, he
agreed to require them to update and balance their cashbooks daily to establish
the correct balance of their accountability as of any given time.

We recommended and Management agreed that, henceforth,
Management strictly adhere with the bonding requirement of all accountable
officers in accordance with Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 dated August 6, 2009
to protect the interest of the government.

We recommended and the Acting Municipal Treasurer agreed to require
the collectors to whom the cash tickets were issued to remit the amount of
₱15,987.00. We also reiterated our previous audit recommendation that
incumbent Acting Acting Municipal Treasurer strictly monitor and control the
purchases and issuances of cash tickets to duly designated revenue collectors by
properly maintaining a permanent record book following the column headings
required under Section 59 (L) of the Local Treasury Manual.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to require all
the PHWs to submit proof of actual exposure to specific health hazards and
occupational risks, and documents required under Section 5.8 of COA Circular
No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to support their hazard pay.

We recommended that, henceforth, Management adhere strictly with the
rates of hazard pay per month, for the actual days of exposure to risks or
danger, as prescribed under Section 3.3 of DBM-DOH Joint Circular No. 1
series of 2016 dated July 15, 2016, to ensure that health workers are justly and
equitably compensated.

We recommended and Management agreed to comply with the required
five days remaining leave credits after monetization in accordance with Section
22 of Rule XVI of the Omnibus Rules Implementing Book V of E.O. No. 292
dated July, 2010. Further, we recommended that Management require the
personnel listed in Annex 10 who availed of monetization of 50% or more of
their accumulated leave credits to submit proof that the proceeds thereof were
used for any of the reasons cited in the Omnibus Rule. Henceforth, we
recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that monetization of leave
credits are properly supported with valid documents in compliance with Section
23 of the Omnibus Rules on Leave and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June
14, 2012 before processing the payment.

We further recommended that VL credits should be exhausted first
before SL credits are used in accordance with CSC Resolution No. 00-0034 dated
January 5, 2000 and that monetization of leave credits shall be availed of only
once a year pursuant to CSC and DBM Joint Circular No. 25-97 dated June 27,
1997.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct all department
heads concerned to submit their respective AAPSI on the CY 2018 AAR, and
assign a personnel to consolidate the same for submission to the Auditor.
Henceforth, we recommended and Management agreed to strictly comply with
the submission of the AAPSI within 60 days upon receipt of the AARs for the
succeeding years.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
GSO to coordinate with the Municipal Engineer to prepare the road map of the municipality to facilitate the preparation of a proper inventory and reporting of the municipality’s road network system;
Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and the GSO with the complete description and cost segregation of the road components for road projects; and thereafter,
Municipal Accountant to record amount of road networks equivalent to the required transition provision percentage every year starting 2016 up to 2019.

We recommended that management, thru the Accounting Office, reclassify the advance payment for mobilization fees recorded in Other Receivables account to Advances to Contractors account, in their respective fund, to conform to the Revised Local Government Chart of Accounts prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-09 dated December 1, 2015, for fair presentation of financial statements.

We recommended that Management return immediately to the source NGAs the idle funds aggregating P 5,159,110.70 pursuant to Section 4 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994 and Section 3.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-01 dated June 14, 2012 on the submission, and liquidation of fund transfer received from National Government Agencies (NGAs) to make these funds available for appropriation to other priority programs, projects and activities of the government.

We recommended that Management fast track the completion of all on-going and unimplemented projects funded under the 20% Development Fund to optimize utilization thereof and fully deliver on time these benefits to the community.
Likewise, the concerned department was advised to monitor regularly the implementation of the 20% Development Plan and closely coordinate with other departments to fully achieve the desired goals and benefits as planned.
We recommended that Management require the contractor to refund the amount of the deficiency or to deduct the amount from any claim of the contractor.

We recommended that management:
Require the contractor to complete the construction of the NCDC project, otherwise, the municipality shall rescind the contract, forfeit the contractor's performance security and takeover the prosecution of the project or award the same to a qualified contractor through negotiated contract. Liquidated damages incurred shall be deducted from the claim of the contractor.
Require the contractor to complete the project of the Concreting of Tabba-Tupanna Road, otherwise, the municipality shall rescind the contract, forfeit the contractor's performance security and takeover the prosecution of the project or award the same to a qualified contractor through negotiated contract.  Liquidated damages incurred including the excess amount of the contract over ABC shall be deducted from the claim of the contractor.

We recommended that Management formulate Policy Guidelines in hiring contract of service employees, taking into consideration the necessity as to the reasonable number of contract of service employees to be assigned in different offices in order to avoid excessive manpower, work duplication and unnecessary expenditures for wages.
Ensure that the policy guidelines formulated would not, in any way, restrict the manpower requirements needed in the delivery of vital services for the implementation of the various programs and activities of the municipality but promote optimum utilization of financial resources.

We recommended and the Management agreed to direct:

the Municipal Accountant to record the appropriate adjusting journal entries to show the accurate balance of the cash in bank account after taking into consideration the necessary supporting documents and computations, if any, as bases in preparing the adjusting journal entries, in conformity with Section 3.3 of COA Circular No. 96-011;

the Municipal Treasurer to send a written notice to the payee of the existence of the checks at least one month before a check becomes stale in accordance with the provision of Section 59 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System; and

the Municipal Accountant to prepare and record the adjusting journal entry in the appropriate agency book to revert back to the appropriate fund the issued check amount and establish the liability of the municipality.

We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the projects officers in charge and undertake necessary actions to enforce collections of these long outstanding receivables.

We recommended and Management agreed to:

require the Municipal Accountant to desist from granting additional cash advance if previous cash advance has not been fully liquidated;

cause the withholding of the payment of the salary if 30 days after due demand, no liquidation or explanation is received from the Accountable Officer, or the explanation received is not satisfactory; and

undertake administrative measures for the recovery and/or settlement of unliquidated cash advances of those accountable officers who are no longer employed with the LGU and to impose the withholding of salaries of those employees with overdue cash advances until the settlement of their cash advance/s.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds and draw the necessary journal entry to correct the balances.

We recommended and the Management agreed to make representation with the concerned National Government Agencies through the Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG) and request for authority to utilize the unused funds to finance other priority projects otherwise return them to the source agencies as mandated.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to:

send confirmation letters to creditors to prove the existence of these liability accounts; and

ensure that claims are substantiated with complete documentation, otherwise revert said balances to Unappropriated Surplus account.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:

direct the Municipal Planning and Development Office (MPDO) and with the GAD Focal Point System to work on the establishment of GAD database to comply with the requirement of Section 37(D), Rule VI of the IRR of RA 9710 thereby ensuring an effective and efficient implementation of GAD;

direct the Municipal Budget Office, Accounting Unit and designated GAD Focal Person to coordinate and properly monitor GAD fund utilization to guide them in the timely and prompt implementation of GAD PPAs; and

propose that the designated GAD Focal Person and as deemed necessary, other employees involved in GAD implementation be able to attend necessary training/webinars on GAD to properly equip them on the implementation of LGUs GAD.
We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the full implementation of the programs, projects and activities covered by the LDRRMF annual plan and budget to achieve its objectives.

We recommended and Management agreed to strategically plan and implement its developmental projects within the target period of accomplishment to ensure that the 20% of the IRA is optimally utilized in the prompt implementation of programs and projects to achieve the desirable socio-economic development as prescribed under DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular 2017-1.

We recommended that Management require the prompt implementation of projects within the budget year so that the desired benefit to the community sought to be established as enshrined in RA 10121 is fully achieved.

We recommended and the Management agreed to strategically plan and direct the Municipal Treasurer to implement strategies to intensify collections and to enforce the tax remedies cited in RA 7160, if necessary.

We recommended that the AAR CY Municipal Agriculturist conduct inventory of breeding stocks and render a report thereon including the fair market value of each stock to the Municipal Accountant, for proper recording in the books of accounts. We recommended further that, henceforth, the Municipal Agriculturist keep a systematic record of births, deaths and disposal of breeding stocks. In case of death or disposal, he shall facilitate the request for relief of accountability in accordance with Sections 151 and 152 of COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992, for dropping the account from the books. We also recommended that a proper disclosure shall be provided in the Notes to the Financial Statements in consonance with PPSAS No. 27 to provide relevant information that might affect the fair presentation of the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Agriculturist submitted a partial list of offspring for CY 2019.
observations.reason: The breeding stock account was still not measured at fair value contrary to paragraph 16 of PPSAS No. 27.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the AAR CY Municipal Accountant (a) conduct a thorough verification and analysis of the dormant receivable accounts and provide for an allowance for impairment in accordance with the PPSAS / PFRS; (b) properly provide disclosure in the Notes to the Financial Statements in consonance with Section 127 of IPSAS No. 1 to provide pertinent information that may affect the fairness of the presentation of the financial statements; and (c) comply with all the conditions and requirements of COA Circular No. 2016-005 for possible filing of request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts by the Local Chief Executive to the Commission on Audit.
observations.managementAction: Allowance for impairment of Other Receivables was provided.
observations.reason: Allowance for impairment of Other Receivables was provided. Receivables amounting to ₱535,627.83. The Municipal Accountant still gathered available documents for possible filing of request for authority to write-off dormant accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the AAR CY Municipal Treasurer prepare the CRAAF monthly duly reconciled with the stocks on hand and to submit a copy to the Auditor in accordance with Section 51 of the NGAs Manual, Volume II.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer submitted the CRAAF only on December 27, 2019.
observations.reason: The CRAAF was not properly prepared and reconciled with the stocks on hand by the Municipal Treasurer as discussed in Finding No. 3, Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management based the fidelity bond of the AO on the total accountability ensuring that cash advances granted do not exceed the maximum accountability, and to renew the same before the expiration date.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer was already increased to ₱500,000.00 effective December 19, 2018 with a minimum and maximum cash accountability of ₱750,001.00 and ₱1,000,000.00 respectively.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality review the availability of the technical personnel, equipment and feasibility of implementing the projects within a realistic timeline in identifying the PPAs to be funded by the 20% DF and, henceforth, hasten the implementation of eligible PPAs to attain socio-economic development as intended in the establishment of the fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer and the designated General Services Officer to ensure that all vehicles and transportation equipment owned by the Municipality are marked with “For Official Use Only” pursuant to Item B of COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977.

observations.recommendation: Required the officials and concerned to return in full the amount received by them as they are not allowed in audit for lack of legal basis.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the chief executive, together with the Sangguniang Bayan, require the refund of the excess amount granted and discontinue the granting of PEI more than the maximum allowable rate of ₱5,000.00 per personnel, as provided for in Section 1.a of E.O. No. 80 dated July 20, 2012, to avoid overpayment.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a. The Municipal Agriculturist supervise the Dispersal Program and see to it that proper records are kept and that all movement (birth of offspring, death, transfers) are completely recorded in their records and reported to the GSO monthly using General Form No. 41 (A) – Property Inventory. b. The Municipal Agriculturist assigned to manage the program keep a record of each animal acquired under the dispersal program, keep systematic records, and report the birth of animals, deaths, and disposals to the GSO monthly using General Form No. 41 (A) – Property Inventory. c. The Municipal Treasurer require the Municipal Agriculturist to submit a monthly report of births of animals and to post these to the property records, after which the same shall be forwarded to the accounting office for posting to the books of accounts. e. The office of the Municipal Agriculturist to update their records and segregate active and inactive contracts. The Municipal Agriculture Office and GSO reconcile their records taking into consideration the latest physical inventory conducted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Municipal Agriculturist agreed to conduct
inventory of breeding stocks and render a report thereon including the fair
market value of each stock to the Municipal Accountant, for proper recording
in the books of accounts. We recommended further that, henceforth, the
Municipal Agriculturist keep a systematic record of births, deaths and
disposal of breeding stocks. In case of death or disposal, he shall facilitate the
request for relief of accountability in accordance with Sections 151 and 152 of
COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992, for dropping the account
from the books.
We also recommended that proper disclosure shall be provided in the
Notes to the Financial Statements in consonance with PPSAS No. 27 to
provide relevant information that might affect the fair presentation of the
financial statements.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to (a) conduct a
thorough verification and analysis of the dormant receivable accounts and
provide for an allowance for impairment in accordance with the PPSAS or
PFRS; (b) properly provide disclosure in the Notes to the Financial Statements
in consonance with Section 127 of IPSAS No. 1 to provide information that
may affect the fairness of the presentation of the financial statements; and (c)
comply with all the conditions and requirements of COA Circular No. 2016-
005 for possible filing of request for authority to write-off dormant receivable
accounts by the Local Chief Executive to the Commission on Audit.

We recommended that Management stop assigning JO personnel as
collecting/disbursing officers since no employer-employee relationship exists
between the LGU and the JOs, and ensure strict adherence to existing laws
and regulations that all accountable officers/employees shall be properly
bonded to safeguard government funds.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to (a) review the
RCDs and see to it that these are completely and properly filled up; (b) check
the accuracy of the accountability for accountable forms by doing physical
count of all accountable forms; and (c) carry forward the correct beginning
balance to the succeeding RCDs.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to prepare the
CRAAF monthly duly reconciled with the stocks on hand and to submit a copy
to the Auditor in accordance with Section 51 of the NGAs Manual, Volume II.

We recommended and the OIC-Municipal Treasurer agreed to require
the Revenue Collection Clerk to submit the RAAF with Money Value,
Invoice and Receipt of Accountable Forms from the date when the Revenue
Collection Clerk handled the cash tickets and account the difference noted
between the RCD and actual cash tickets on hand to properly establish her
accountability.
We also recommended and the OIC-Municipal Treasurer agreed that,
henceforth, she shall strictly monitor and control the purchases and issuances
of accountable forms with money value, like cash tickets, by maintaining a
permanent record book following the column headings required under Section
59(L) of the Local Treasury Manual. The Municipality is likewise required to
devise their own system in the issuance of cash tickets to ensure proper control,
accounting and audit. Control measures enumerated in Section 59(C) of the
Local Treasury Manual include but not limited to the following:
a. Stamping serial numbers or the name or logo of the Municipality at
the back of the cash tickets before issuing to assigned collectors;
b. Affixing of initials of collector and date of issuance in the cash tickets
before issuance; and
c. Tearing off of cash tickets after collections have been received to avoid
possible re-issuance.

We recommended and Management agreed that the fidelity bond of the
AO should be based on the total accountability ensuring that cash advances
granted do not exceed the maximum accountability, and to renew the same
before the expiration date.

We recommended that the Municipality appropriate and utilize the 20%
Development Fund only for viable and sustainable development programs and
projects enumerated under DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No.
2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.
During the exit conference, the Municipal Budget Officer commented that just
like the Construction and Rehabilitation of Health Centers, the Construction of
Day Care Centers are also social services projects, hence, could be considered as
development projects properly chargeable to the 20% DF.
It is emphasized that the priority development projects chargeable to the 20%
DF are enumerated under the aforementioned Joint Memorandum Circular.
Applying therefore the legal maxim “expression unios est exclusion alterius”
which means the expression of one thing is the exclusion of the other, those not
included as allowable development projects chargeable against the 20% DF
enumerated under Item 3.0 of the said JMC are deemed excluded.
Attention is also invited to Section 5.0 of the same Circular which provides
that the “xxx. The utilization of the 20% DF, whether willfully or through
negligence, for any purpose other than those expressly prescribed by law or public
policy shall be subject to the sanctions provided under RA No. 7160 and other
applicable laws.” (emphasis supplied)
However, the foregoing PPAs can be sourced from the General Fund-Proper
of the Municipality since these are among the basic services and facilities provided
under Section 17 of the Local Government Code.

We recommended that the Municipality review the availability of the
technical personnel, equipment and feasibility of implementing the projects
within a realistic timeline in identifying the PPAs to be funded by the 20% DF
and, henceforth, hasten the implementation of eligible PPAs to attain socio-
economic development as intended in the establishment of the fund.
The Municipal Engineer in her written reply informed the Audit Team that the
projects under the 20% DF for CY 2018 were not optimally utilized during the year
because of the implementation of the CY 2017 20% DF projects. Also, priority
was given on the implementation of the Salintubig and BUB projects. However,
Management agreed to expedite the implementation of the projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MSWDO to
carefully plan the procurement of food items for the Supplemental Feeding
Program and for the BAC to undertake the procurement thru competitive
bidding in accordance with Section 10, Rule IV of the IRR of RA No. 9184,
except as provided in Rule XVI of the IRR.
Management informed the Audit Team that the food items purchased for the
SFP for day care children were procured through shopping because no eligible
bidders responded to the invitation to bid (ITB). The BAC also considered the fact
that these items could not be purchased in bulk because these are perishable foods
that could not be stocked for a longer period of time.
Contrary to the comment of Management, there was no bidding conducted
because the BAC in its resolutions attached to the liquidation reports of the
transferred funds, recommended for the purchase of the food items through
shopping. Moreover, while it is true that food items are perishable goods, they can
still be procured thru competitive bidding because delivery terms can be arranged
with the winning supplier in time for the feeding schedule. The delivery schedule
should be indicated in the ITB, PRs as well as in the POs.

We recommended and the MSWDO agreed to prepare the PRs for the
procurement of food items based on the approved cycle menu (Annex A of AO
No. 04, series of 2016). If there be any modification thereof, the MSWDO is
likewise required to attach in the DV the duly reviewed and approved menu by
the MNAO/Nutritionists of the Field Office so that purchases of ingredients
could be properly evaluated.
We further recommended that the Accounting Office see to it that
disbursement vouchers covering payments for the procured items are
supported with complete documentation, including the daily attendance.
The MSWDO commented that aside from the food items purchased, the
mothers of the children beneficiaries have prepared and cooked vegetables which
they brought from their backyard garden so the children could have vegetable soup.

We recommended and Management agreed to submit the special project
plan showing the outputs or deliverables per project component of each
members of the MCT to determine the estimated man-hours spent by the latter
which shall serve as basis in the computation of their honorarium and the
other aforementioned lacking documents required under COA Circular No.
2012-1. Henceforth, ensure that the disbursement vouchers and payrolls are
supported with complete documentary requirements to establish the validity of
the transactions made.

We recommended that the Municipality optimally utilize the 70%
LDRRMF for expenditures that would strengthen its capacity to avoid or
mitigate the adverse effects of disasters or calamity as provided under RA No.
10121 and NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 2013-1. Expenditures which
are administrative in nature shall be properly charged against its regular
budget under the General Fund Proper.
The LDRRMO commented that the purchase of one unit ambulance was
charged against the LDRRMF because the said vehicle, aside from being used as
emergency transportation, is also used for DRRM operations. She also added that
the remunerations of the Community Based FMR workers were charged to the
LDRRMF because management believed that maintaining and declogging of canal,
clearing of bushes and cutting of trees would lessen possible damages during rainy
season. The workers were also standby search and rescue volunteer in their
respective barangays.
As already discussed, Sections 4.3 and 4.4 of NDRRMC-DILG-DBM and
CSC JMC No. 2014-1 provide that the budgetary requirements for personal
services, maintenance and other operating expenditures and capital outlay of the
LDRRMO which are not provided under NDRRMC-DBM and DILG Memorandum
Circular No. 2013-1 and COA Circular No. 2012-002 shall be sourced from the
General Fund of the Municipality. Therefore, the work performed by the
Community Based FMR workers should have been charged to the General Fund
Proper.
On the other hand, the purchase of ambulance, although can be used for the
LDRRMO operations, but most of the time is being used in the normal day-to-day
operations to transport patients, thus, are properly chargeable to the 20% DF under
Social Services.

We recommended and Management agreed that the SWMB review and
update the MSWMP as required under Section 16 of RA No. 9003 for
purposes of ensuring its sustainability, viability, effectiveness and relevance,
and to submit the same to the NSWMC for review and approval.
We also recommended that the SWMB prepare plans for safe closure
and rehabilitation of the existing open dump facility and the implementation of

Audit Team that they personally submitted the SWMP to the Environment
Management Bureau, Region VII, Cebu City on February 21, 2019 and are
currently revising the plan with the assistance of the ENRD Provincial Office.

Waste Management Board ensure the implementation and compliance of the

Formulate and implement a comprehensive and integrated LDRRMP in accordance with the national, regional, and provincial framework, and policies on disaster risk reduction in close coordination with the local development councils (LDCs);

Prepare and submit to the local sanggunian through the LDRRMC and the LDC the annual LDRRMO Plan and budget, the proposed programming of the LDRRMF, other dedicated disaster risk reduction and management resources, and other regular funding source/s and budgetary support of the LDRRMO;

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a) The accounting office and GSO reconcile their records of unserviceable properties. Also, the Municipal Treasurer should prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) with the appraisal of each item conducted by an appraisal committee. b) Management undertake the immediate disposal of the unserviceable properties following the procedures and guidelines provided under Section 79 of Presidential Decree 1445 and Sections 501-510 of the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual, Volume I. c) The accountant reclassify the unserviceable properties based on the IIRUP to Other Assets account for a fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements. d) The accountant drop the amount of the unserviceable properties from their books of accounts after disposal, and records the proceeds of the auction sale, if any.
observations.reason: The GSO has not yet updated the records of unserviceable properties. Also, the Municipal Treasurer has not yet finished the preparation of the Inventory and Inspection Reports of Unserviceable Properties because of the volume of unserviceable properties that have accumulated thru time.

observations.recommendation: Direct the Treasury, Accounting and Legal Offices to secure the Land Titles to all real properties as an indispensable requirement. Lands owned or acquired by LGUs without Torrens Title shall within the next fiscal year be asked to be surveyed with a view to securing title thereto, and the appropriation for such survey and other requirements for securing title shall be deemed a preferred charge against the unappropriated funds during the said ensuing fiscal year (Sec. 955 of COA Circular No. 92-386). Instruct the Legal Office to facilitate the application for the issuance of the TCT in favour of the Municipality of Pamplona so that government may be shielded from lawsuits or unlawful claims over such property. Subsequently, all purchases of real property should be done in accordance with the pertinent laws governing such transactions in order to prevent disallowances in audit. Direct the Accountant and the Assessor to reconcile both their records so that these should come into agreement for the Land account to be fairly presented in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Local Chief Executive create committee to conduct physical inventory of the properties of the municipality and to prepare a report thereon to be submitted to the Municipal Accountant for reconciliation with that of the accounting records, copy furnished the auditor concerned pursuant to COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992 and Section 122 of P. D. 1445.
The management agreed to adhere with the audit recommendations. They commented that the physical inventory taking is in on-going. The GSO and Municipal Accountant raised that there are some PPE which do not have pertinent documents, hence the value could not be determined. The Audit Team suggested that Management create an appraisal committee.

We recommended management to create a committee composed of members from the offices of the Municipal Treasurer, Accountant and Assessor with the primary objective of tracing back prior year’s records/documents and reconcile the same. Effect the necessary adjustment(s) in the books of accounts to provide the accurate balance of the RPT/SET Receivables in the financial statements.
The recommendations were well taken by Management. The Management commented that the Office of the Municipal Treasurer and Assessor need additional personnel in updating the list of taxpayers and delinquent taxpayers.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the accountable officer to restitute immediately the missing funds and to immediately relieve the Municipal Treasurer from his duties and responsibilities without prejudice to the filing of criminal and administrative cases to effect the recovery of the amount of shortage noted in audit. Likewise, withhold the payment of salary and other emoluments due to the Municipal Treasurer pursuant to Section 37 of P. D. 1445.
The Municipal Treasurer submitted a letter-reply on October 26, 2016. He pleaded to be given an ample time to restitute the said shortage so as to collect cash vales from various officials and employees. On December 6, 2016, he submitted to the Office of the Auditor his various deposits to the authorized depository bank of the Municipality amounting to P1,090,942.52, which represents his cash shortage.
Likewise, the Honorable Mayor designated Ms. Catherine Guddaran, Revenue Collection Clerk III, as liquidating officer effective November 14, 2016 who is responsible in preparing report of collections and depositing the same on the Municipality’s authorized depository bank

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare and submit regularly to the Office of the Auditor Monthly Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms With and Without Money Value to ensure compliance with the aforecited regulation and to facilitate inventory of accountable forms.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer to locate the missing accountable forms. Likewise, the collecting officers to whom the missing accountable forms were issued should likewise be held accountable.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned official/employees to liquidate immediately their outstanding cash advances and ensure compliance with the above mentioned rules and regulations on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances. Also, we recommended the Local Chief Executive to order the withholding of payment of any money due to the above-named employees pursuant to Section 9.3.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002
The recommendation was well taken by Management.

We recommended that Management refrain from paying overtime services without appropriation. Comply strictly with the provisions of the CSC and DBM Joint Circular No. 1 s. 2015 dated November 25, 2015.
The recommendation was well taken. Management assured the Audit Team that they already stopped the above practice.

8.6.	With the above observations, we recommended that:
Management attribute the hiring of Job Order personnel to specific projects for a period of not exceeding six months.  Maximize personnel holding permanent positions to do regular, administrative and clerical functions.
Management formulate a Program/Policy Guidelines in the hiring of job order personnel, taking into consideration the necessity of hiring and the reasonable number of job order employees to be assigned in the different offices to avoid excessive manpower and work duplication.
8.7.	The recommendations were well taken by Management. They commented that the drafting of policy/guidelines is on-going.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2009-001 on submission of copies of perfected contracts, purchase orders or job orders within five (5) days after their execution and notify the Audit Team the time and date of the scheduled deliveries by furnishing copies of delivery documents of goods within twenty-four (24) hours after acceptance in compliance to Section 6.06 of COA Circular No. 95-006 and COA Circular No. 2009-002.
The Management agreed to adhere with the recommendations. The GSO and the Municipal Accountant discussed procurement process in order to come out with a centralized procurement plan.

We recommended that management carefully plan the implementation of projects to fast track the delivery of basic services most especially the intended beneficiaries of the project.
The recommendation was well taken by Management. The project is on-going.

We recommended the management to fill up the created LDRRMO position in order to strengthen the LGU’s capacity for the disaster risk reduction and management.
The recommendation was well taken by the Management. They commented that the funding on the salaries, wages and other benefits of the DRRM Assistant was already included in the annual budget for CY 2017. The LCE explained that the LDRRMO position was not yet included because of PS Limitation. Meanwhile, the HRMO – Designate commented that posting of the vacancy will be facilitated.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (LDRRMC) to closely monitor and evaluate the utilization of the LDRRMF to ensure timely implementation of the pre-disaster preparedness programs, projects and activities.
Likewise, the unexpended balances of the LDRRMF may be released and utilized for new expenditure items upon submission of a revised AIP as approved by the local sanggunian and Local Chief Executive (LCE) upon recommendation of the LDRRMC in accordance with the approved LDRRMP as provided in the Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 of the NDRRMC, DBM and DILG.
The recommendations were well taken by Management. The unimplemented PPA from the current appropriation was the construction of bridge. They explained that the DPWH recommended that the implementation of the said project will be done on summer. Meanwhile, the continuing appropriation for the 70% Mitigation Fund was already reprogrammed.

We recommended that Management require the contractors to undertake the necessary repairs to correct the above deficiencies at their own expenses pursuant to Section 62 – Warranty of Republic Act 9184, otherwise, the amounts representing the costs of the cracks shall be deducted from any claim of the contractors from the Municipality.
We recommended that management require the contractors, OCP Construction and PP Eslabon Hardware & Construction to refund the amount of the above deficiencies amounting to P147,982.80, P21,943.96 and P44,990.05, respectively,  or it  shall be deducted from any claim of the contractors from the Municipality.
We recommended that Management instruct the concerned employees to submit to the Office of the Auditor the Statement of Work Accomplished at the expiry date of the contracts of the above infrastructure projects for proper evaluation and computation of liquidated damages (LD) per Annex “E” 8.1 of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. 9184.
The Municipal Engineer explained that the delays incurred by the contractors were due to bad weather. However, during the course of the audit, there were no document/records attached to the disbursement vouchers that would indicate that there were work suspensions. The Municipal Engineer committed to submit the related documents which will be subjected for evaluation.

We recommend that Management create a Solid Waste Management Board to prepare a 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan consistent with the National Solid Waste Management Framework and submit the same to the Solid Waste Management Commission for approval as required by Republic Act No. 9003, otherwise known as the Ecological Solid Waste Management Act of 2000.
The Management agreed to the audit recommendations. The LCE commented that the she is in the process of reviewing the qualified persons suited to be part of the Solid Waste Management Board. Also, the Municipality already requested technical support for the formulation of the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan.

We recommended that Management ensure the timely implementation of the programs, projects and activities under the Gender and Development Fund to extend to the constituents particularly the women and children their immediate needs as enumerated under the Magna Carta of Women Law.
The recommendation was well taken by Management. They explained that the unimplemented PPA (Infrastructure Asset) was attributed to the 20% Development Fund.

It has to incorporate those activities within the Municipality and those that are in accordance with GAD laws and guidelines, such as the Magna Carta of Women, Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of the PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA, the Philippine Plan for Gender and Development, rules and regulations issued by the Philippine Commission on Women, etc. Moreover, management shall see to it that its GPB shall be submitted to the DILG and/or PCW for approval, in order to ensure that GAD issues and concerns are addressed.
observations.managementAction: Various related programs, projects and activities were mainstreamed and included in the GAD Plan.

No. 80 dated July 20, 2012 to avoid overpayment.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a) Stop the practice of charging prior years’ electricity expenses against the appropriation of the year it was paid. b) If funds are available, require the Sangguniang Bayan to make a supplemental budget so that unpaid obligations can be settled properly. c) Exercise prudence in spending the municipal funds. Exert effort to control the use of electricity so that the municipality can avoid problems in paying the obligation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a. The Municipal Agriculturist supervise closely the Dispersal Program and see to it that proper records are kept and that all movement (birth of offspring, death, transfers) are completely recorded in their records and reported to the municipal treasurer. b. The Municipal Agriculturist assigned to manage the program keep a record of each animal acquired under the dispersal program, keep systematic records, and report birth of animals, deaths and disposals to the GSO monthly using General Form No. 41 (A) – Property Inventory. c. In case of death or disposal, the Municipal Agriculturist be required to facilitate the request for relief of accountability, or to require payment of the value of the animal if warranted under the circumstances, in order that accountability and liability can be imposed. d. The Municipal Treasurer require the Municipal Agriculturist to submit a monthly report of births of animals and to post these to the property records, after which the same shall be forwarded to the accounting office for posting to the books of accounts. e. The office of the Municipal Agriculturist to update their records and segregate active and inactive contracts. f. The Municipal Agriculture Office and GSO reconcile their records taking into consideration the latest physical inventory conducted.
observations.reason: The Inventory Team has not yet supervised/inventoried the supervising livestock/animals. Also, the Treasurer’s Office and Accounting Office have not yet updated their records because the office of the Municipal Agriculturist is still retrieving and organizing its records.

observations.recommendation:

We also  recommended that Management desist from approving expenditures which are not legitimate charges against the SEF to prevent suspension or disallowance in audit.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to designate a full time Liquidating Officer (LO) who shall efficiently receive and verify the daily collections and remittances of all collectors.

observations.recommendation:

The PPMP output shall then be submitted to the Budget Officer for her evaluation whether it is within the approved budget of the corresponding end-user unit/office.

We also  recommended  that Management direct the BAC Secretariat to consolidate the APP based from the PPMP of each end-user unit and ensure that the preferred goods/services to be purchased as indicated in the PPMP have detailed descriptions  to _ facilitate _easy consolidation as well as fast processing of its procurement by the GSO.

observations.recommendation:
The Municipal Treasurer, as the supervisor of the Collecting Officer should install sound internal control in the collection and remittance of government funds in accordance with his powers and duties as provided under Section 470 (d)(2) of the Local Government Code of 1991;

The Municipal Treasurer sees to it that all collections are reported/accounted on the actual dates they transpired and those taxes, fees and charges collected for the fiscal year should be duly accounted and reported during the fiscal year.

The Liquidating Officer should strictly monitor the remittance of every Revenue Collector by signing the last Accountable Form included in the remittance. All Official Receipt issued to the Collecting Officer must be presented to the Liquidating Officer for verification to deter delayed remittance and other forms of fraudulent acts of collection officers.

The personnel in-charge of releasing the accountable forms should require the presentation of the used stub of official receipt and the approval of the Municipal Treasurer/Liquidating Officer before releasing additional accountable forms.

File appropriate disciplinary actions against erring collectors as maybe warranted under the law.
observations.managementAction: Retirement  of the Municipal Treasurer.
observations.reason: Restated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation:
Require the Municipal Waste Management Board to prepare, submit and implement the 10-year SWMP for the safe and sanitary management of solid waste generation in areas under its geographic and political coverage. The plan shall be subjected to the approval of the National Solid Waste Management Commission (NSWMC) and shall be consistent with the national franiework and in accordarics with the Act and of the policies set by the NSWMC.

The Municipality is required to have its own dumpsite that is compliant with the standards of the government.

The Local Government of Pamplona, Camarines Sur is requested to provide sufficient appropriations for Solid Waste Management Program and implement what was approved by the MDC in furtherance with the purpose/objectives of RA 9003.

The Municipality was not able to formulate a 10-year SWMP but provided an appropriation from EDF for Solid Waste Management programs in 2015 & 2016,
observations.managementAction: The Municipality was not able to formulate a 10-year SWMP but provided an appropriation from EDF for Solid Waste Management programs in 2015 & 2016,
